comments.title: Ogham Sanity Check, Two Currents, Japanese Stuff, Meditation Symbols
comments.content:
Howdy,

Thanks again for everything you do hosting this and your other spaces, as well as writing so many thought-provoking books.

1) Still another question on an Ogham divination about an SOP intent. This time, I'm trying to work out what to do for an external-facing intent. I got a favorable reading for a general class of goals, then for several days asked about more specific goals within that category, and yesterday I got a reading that seemed pretty straightforward in terms of the components, but still left me scratching my head a bit on the overall interpretation. I asked "what do I most need to know about [intent]?" and got:
- Setting: Muin (Reversed)
- Working: Beith
- Outcome: Koad (Reversed)

To me, this seems to say "you've been working on things the wrong way, this working would give you a new start, but there's no way to tell where it will lead". That strikes me as a lukewarm positive reading, but I wanted to make sure that Koad (Reversed) wasn't a screaming red flag that I was missing.

2) If working through the DMH, are imaginal experiences of the solar and telluric currents while meditating/scrying a sign that one ought to start thinking about the rituals for awakening the currents?

3) Given your fondness for at least some things Japanese, do you have any thoughts on how well (if at all) the Book of Five Rings can inform Western uses of the Five elements?

4) Another Japanese-ish question: What are your thoughts on the spiritual/esoteric value of Zen and the Art of Motorcycle Maintenance?

5) I recently read Dion Fortune's "Helps for Meditation" in Aspects of Occultism. In it, she recommends a symbol as an aid to meditation. I haven't encountered this advice in any of your writing thus far (to be fair, I haven't read any of your classic GD books), so I wondered your thoughts on it - I suspect a large part of the answer is "experiment and see what works for you", but if you have thoughts on whether or not such symbols are useful and if so what their characteristics ought to be, I'd be very interested to hear.

I appreciate your time and thought,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Ogham Sanity Check, Two Currents, Japanese Stuff, Meditation Symbols
replies.content:
1) I tend to read Koad reversed as "reply hazy, ask again later."

2) Yes, though you should check with divination as well.

3) My copy of Gorin no Sho has been with me longer than almost any other book I own. Yes, it uses the same set of elements that Western magic does, rather than the very different Chinese set.

4) I thought it was worth reading, but only as one among a few dozen other interesting books.

5) That's squarely in the "experiment and see what works for you" camp. I don't use one, but I know people who do.

replies.title: Re: Ogham Sanity Check, Two Currents, Japanese Stuff, Meditation Symbols
replies.content:
Thanks very much!

On 3), do you think that Musashi was influenced by any Western occult sources? I've never heard of anything besides Jesuit-flavored Catholicism coming into Japan with the Portuguese before Tokugawa closed the island to westerners, but I've never much looked into it, and definitely not since learning more about occultism. I also haven't read it recently, so I don't know what kind of clues are in the text.

3b) Any preferences for translation? I have D.E. Tarver's translation, which purports to bring a martial artist's perspective to the text, but I know that choice of translation makes a big difference with foreign-language works.

replies.title: Re: Ogham Sanity Check, Two Currents, Japanese Stuff, Meditation Symbols
replies.content:
3) No, the esoteric Buddhist denominations in Japan use the same five-element system we use in the West, which they got from India ultimately. Japanese occultism has used that system extensively since ancient times.

4) I don't know enough about Japanese to be able to judge; the edition I have is by Victor Harris; I recommend getting two or three translations and comparing them to get some sense of what the author is saying.

replies.title: Re: Ogham Sanity Check, Two Currents, Japanese Stuff, Meditation Symbols
replies.content:
3) Fascinating! I had mostly assumed the 4/5/7 element system was ultimately derived from the Greeks, but my knowledge of Indian sources is spotty at best. And any time Indian religion is a source, it makes me wonder what might go back to Proto-Indo-European belief.

I'm currently working my way through Manly P. Hall's Man: The Grand Symbol of the Mysteries, which does a great job of highlighting parallels between different traditions.

Whichever way(s) I choose to follow up, I suspect there will be lots of fruitful threads to follow for years to come.

replies.title: Re: Ogham Sanity Check, Two Currents, Japanese Stuff, Meditation Symbols
replies.content: 3) It's anyone's guess who thought of it first, or if it was independently invented in more than one place. You can tell that Musashi's version has Indian antecedents because he calls the fifth element Void -- that's the Japanese version of the Chinese version of the Sanskrit term akasha, which originally meant "sky" or "space."

replies.title: Re: Ogham Sanity Check, Two Currents, Japanese Stuff, Meditation Symbols
replies.content:
Thanks for the question about the Book of Five rings, this was a connection I had not made.

Perhaps of interest to you...
Once upon a long drive I happened upon a podcast called Heretics by Woven Energy, and then also the Woven Energy podcast.
They discuss many cultures including the ancient norse and Japanese. Somewhere in there, on a talk about aikido I believe (I play Tomiki Aikido,) there was a discussion about the old religions of Japan having a surprisingly similar creation story to the old norse, including a formulation of reality with 9 worlds housing different beings.

I believed myself split in my interests - western occultism, Japanese martial arts and religion, and norse mythology and religion; but it seems the threads connect regardless of my division.

replies.title: Re: Ogham Sanity Check, Two Currents, Japanese Stuff, Meditation Symbols
replies.content:
Fascinating! Thanks very much for sharing. I had read that many followers of northern polytheism found a certain kinship with Shinto, but I hadn't heard about the creation myth. I'll have to check that out.

I recently (re)-read this discussion of the Proto-Indo-European creation myth(s): http://piereligion.org/yamamyth.html

That site argues that the Semitic creation myth(s) might have been influenced by early contact with Proto-Indo-European speakers, which is a super interesting possibility, but awfully hard to know much about besides tantalizing glimpses.

Now, to draw these two threads possibly together: there are various reasons to link early Proto-Indo-Europeans to the circumpolar peoples, both through culture and ancient DNA samples. It occurs to me that the indigenous people of Japan, the Ainu, might also be linked (though I don't know as much about this) and that there could be an influence there.

I'll have to do some digging, so thanks again for the new path to explore.

replies.title: Re: Ogham Sanity Check, Two Currents, Japanese Stuff, Meditation Symbols
replies.content: 2. If it helps, when I came upon the chapter on awakening the currents in DMH, I sort of jokingly asked the ogham if I am ready (fully expecting to get an answer to be patient). The ogham said go ahead. And I pretty much asked again “really?” And got the answer “Yes really”. So I started the preparations and did it. I encourage you to do the divination.

replies.title: Re: Ogham Sanity Check, Two Currents, Japanese Stuff, Meditation Symbols
replies.content: Thanks very much for the data point, I'll start consulting the Ogham on that.

replies.title: Re: Ogham Sanity Check, Two Currents, Japanese Stuff, Meditation Symbols
replies.content:
(4) My two cents' worth. In Lila, the sequel to ZAMM, Robert Pirsig elaborates a metaphysical system that says everything is made out of Quality. (He starts down this road in ZAMM but doesn't get very far.) I can't think of any explicit connections that he draws to occultism, but note that Quality (by definition) involves the perception that something is good. So to say the whole world is made of Quality is formally equivalent to saying that everything is conscious, although rocks and humans and ideas all exhibit their respective consciousnesses on different levels somehow.

Quite apart from any esoteric applications, I really enjoyed both books. Lila is more explicitly metaphysical, but by all means read ZAMM first: the narrative voice is more engaging, and you'll be able to decide if you like this fellow before following him into drier and more arid reaches of thought.

Michael

replies.title: Re: Ogham Sanity Check, Two Currents, Japanese Stuff, Meditation Symbols
replies.content:
Thanks very much! I've read and enjoyed ZAMM a couple of times, but not since I began learning about occultism. Maybe it's time for a revisit.

And Lila's been on my list for a while now, thank you for the recommendation, I'll bump it closer to the top!

comments.title: magical insurance policies
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Where I live it is compulsory to buy a certain type of third party insurance, informally called a green slip, in order to register a motor vehicle. One year a relative was sent quotes by three companies. Not understanding the system she thought they were all invoices and got triple the insurance she needed. Had there been an accident in which her car had injured somebody, the triple insurance would have been moot. No question of 3x the payout of the claim.

In the realm of magical protection, I am hoping to ensure I do not suffer a like misunderstanding.

A friend has displeased a charismatic acquaintance who is undoubtedly psychopathic. She has described books he owns and rituals he does, which amount to black magic. He has been trying to smear her professional reputation despite having been a happy acupuncture patient of hers. I offered to pass on some of the protective practices you have taught us, John, and then realized I need to be able to distinguish between them so I don't give her all green slips and miss the property damage and comprehensive insurance.

So if this is not too much of a mess, would you mind please pointing out what I have wrong or right in this classification?:
- against etheric attacks: bowls of salt and of vinegar, salt & nail amulets, Judson exercise, geometric designs such as hexafoils, plants and stones listed in the ENM.
(Does natural magic (eg protective plants and stones) in general operate at this level?)
- for astral attacks: Sphere of Protection or Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram with Middle Pillar.

I know that if the magician is competent enough in relation to you these won't dispel curses. I have been doing the LRP and MPE since you posted the polytheist versions on dreamwidth. When my manager left his job suddenly two years ago, shortly after I had been making complaints about him, I suffered six months of idiopathic illness which the doctors were unable to diagnose. Divination confirmed that man was behind it. And I don't know how powerful a magician the current antagonist is. Even one of your correspondents who is, IIRC, a Druid wrote on MM some time back that attacks on him and his wife were getting through. (If you are reading this, sir, I'd welcome any comments on what you have learned about that situation since.)

Warm regards and sincere thanks,

iridescent scintillating elver

replies.title: Re: magical insurance policies
replies.content:
1) Your analysis is basically right, though there's some overlap -- for example, the Judson exercise is primarily etheric but has some astral effect as well.

2) You might want to add hoodoo baths to the list of protections -- they're covered in the Magic Monday FAQ -- and also consider sprinkling pepper in your shoes. (It can go under the insole.) That's another classic hoodoo method for dispelling nasty energies.

replies.title: Re: magical insurance policies
replies.content: 2) How often should the pepper be replaced?

replies.title: Re: magical insurance policies
replies.content: Depends on how much noxious magic is being sent your way. Once a week is a good minimum, once a day may be necessary when conditions are bad.

replies.title: Re: magical insurance policies
replies.content: Ah yes, the hoodoo stuff I'd quite forgotten about. Thank you again.

comments.title: A little help with a recent divination

I performed a tarot reading earlier today. It was prompted by a most unusual occurrence at my home shrine. I had just finished my morning offerings and devotions, and I used a candle snuffer to put out each candle. I'm certain they were all out. I'm rather paranoid about leaving burning candles unattended, so I do not think I'd have missed one. I walked off to continue my day, then passed by my shrine a few minutes later. None explicitly magical, but three things stand out to me from your description: 1) That you've had rather a lot of sexual encounters with the same issues, 2) that she has another current partner, and 3) your description of the apparent mismatch between physical and emotional connection.

1) As another commenter said, if y'all have been together 50 times and only 2 of them have done much for her, it might just be that you aren't physically compatible. There can be all kinds of reasons to be attracted to someone "on paper", but actually getting physically excited depends on a lot of stuff we don't have much conscious control over, and maybe you two don't match up as much as either of you would like.

2) Not to be a prude, but in most cultures in most times, it is far rarer for women to have multiple current partners than for men to do so. More usually, if a woman is with more than one man at the same time, she is evaluating switching from one to the other, or she is getting something different from each. For example, a woman might marry a rich, high-status man for the security and have occasional sex with the handsome gardnerer for the excitement. If your partner has told you that she's getting more sexual gratification from the other fellow, you might be fulfilling a different need for her, which may or may not be what you're looking for from her.

3) You said that a) the times that have been better have involved small amounts of alcohol for one or both of you, and b) that she says she cares for you more emotionally than past partners who have been more physically gratifying. This suggests that there's some kind of mismatch between what she/you consciously think/feel about the relationship and what is going on emotionally. Alcohol lowers inhibitions, and as mentioned above, a lot of times we have thoughts/feelings that make sense rationally, but that don't necessarily line up with what our bodies want or need. This implies to me that she might see you as a good partner rationally, but some part of her has some hang ups that relaxing or "cutting loose" helps her get past. comments.title: Manly P Hall
comments.content:
Morning John,
I’ve reached the final article in the Manly P Hall series, Disciplines of Meditation and Realization.  https://manlyphall.info/a-monthly-letter/4107-Disciplines12.pdf
I have found them inspiring, thought provoking and transformative.  They will provide meditative material that will last years, and I can’t thank you enough for giving us the link.  I have also found them a perfect accompaniment to the OBOD course, as I near the end of the Ovate stage.  However, there are a few thoughts or observations that I have developed, not as a criticism I may add.  I note that Hall does not seem to embrace the use of deity in the journey of the Self.  For me, deity has an important role in improving the efficiency of the journey.  Hall’s preference seems to be one of letting the journey be as long as it needs in unfolding the Self.  Clearly there is more than one path, but I prefer one that involves the assistance of someone who has more wisdom than I to allow me to learn with hopefully less of the classic blunders along the way!  
Secondly, I found myself at odds with this statement, “He must fight without fighting.  For he opposes ignorance with any impulse of the will, his realisation fades away and he is left helpless.” Now, I may be reading it wrong, but to me will power is key to the journey to enlightenment.  The Cosmic Doctrine looks at the importance of restraint, and restraint requires will power.  If one is to leave form behind and become formless, it is important to learn the value of self-restraint, and self restraint requires will power.  Hall seems to be suggesting, that realisation overcomes “shadows and unrealities” as “light comes, darkness fades away”, and that implies that will power is not required.  That doesn’t resonate with me at all.   Perhaps this is another example of different paths for different folks, but I’d appreciate your thoughts on this apparent contradiction.
Regards
Averagejoe

replies.title: Re: Manly P Hall
replies.content:
In several places in his writings, Hall stresses the point that different people naturally experience spiritual realities in different ways; he notes in at least one place that this can be a guide to what traditions you've studied and practiced in previous lives. He himself was most comfortable with impersonal forms of the divine, but he was aware that some people prefer to work with divine persons instead.

As for the role of will, to my mind that's one of the weaknesses in his teaching, but then I also belong on the Dion Fortune end of the spectrum! Here again, some people find it useful to train and develop the will, while others find this a distraction. But then Hall was never a dogmatist. He said it the way he saw it, but he was always careful to note that there are many paths, and his philosophical approach was only one of those paths.

For what it's worth, btw, I think you're quite correct that his book meshes extremely well with the OBOD course! You could very easily fit his practice in with the OBOD inner grove and light-body practices, and the results would likely be very good indeed.

replies.content:
Possession is a very distinctive thing. It involves a complete change in consciousness; the face is typically distorted, the voice changes to something wholly unfamiliar, and in general, the possessed person acts as tnough what's inside is not human. A trance state with typically human states of fear and rage, projected irrationally on others, is nothing like extreme enough.

Human beings can get very, very crazy when they're caught up in extreme cognitive dissonance, especially when they're desperately trying to insist that they haven't become everything they claim to hate. One of the things that comes into play in that sort of situation, when you have a lot of people caught up in the same state, is that a groupmind is formed -- basically a pattern of consciousness that reinforces the emotional and cognitive state that created it. Most examples of really disastrous mass events involve the creation of a groupmind -- and yes, it can override the thinking processes of people who feed it, and get them to behave in astonishingly self-defeating and self-destructive ways.

replies.title: (no subject)

An egregor is a slower and usually a more deliberate structure, and it draws as much of its force from shared thoughts as from shared feelings. replies.title: Re: Various
replies.content:
It wasn't late at all -- it landed here at 6:07 AM local time. I hope you and yours are okay!

1) Some varieties of trees thrive when cut. In western Washington, where I grew up, vine maple was a great example. You didn't cut it, or even trim it, because if you did, it sent up a dozen new shoots from the base, and it'll keep doing more and more of that the more you cut it -- think of a vegetable hydra. This example probably started out as four trees that somebody tried to trim.

[IMAGE:0]

So if you want to coppice, you choose a tree that likes to do that. As for your specific question, you're talking partly to the nature spirits, partly to the group soul of that species, and partly to the tree itself, which isn't sentient the way we are but has its own, very different kind of consciousness. If you have a variety that thrives with such treatment, the spirits will be fine with it, especially if you let them know what you have in mind and tend the tree carefully when and after you cut it.

2) All this is quite normal. Ordinary human consciousness is best described as a shallow doze -- and yes, it can take a while for relaxation to set in.

3) If Knight doesn't specify, it's up to you!

replies.content:
A few thoughts on reducing social media use, to be taken or left as you like:

Cal Newport has a book called Digital Minimalism which is about how to be more intentional with your use of digital technologies. His basic approach is to take a month (so Lent would likely work as well!) to not do any non-essential social media, email, websites, and so forth, and at the end of this "fast", only come back to the stuff that does more good for you than it costs. He strongly recommends that you not only think about "cutting out" digital stuff you don't want, but that you also think about what you will do instead: read more books? Pick up a craft? See your friends/family more in person? Otherwise, you end up sitting around wondering what to do, and your old default answer of "check Facebook/Instagram/Reddit/Etc" seems really tempting.

Perhaps less necessary for giving up social media for Lent, but some maybe helpful tips for the longer term:
1) Uninstall social media from your phone: the social media companies put far more time and money into making their mobile apps addictive than they do their computer web browser interfaces. Also, we tend to use our phones more habitually, as a way to fill otherwise empty time, whereas it's often easier to treat what you do on your computer more intentionally. This extra bit of friction helps to be more intentional about "do I really want to check Facebook right now?"

2) Turn off notifications: This is especially true of "Push Notifications" like badges and alerts on your phone, but I'd recommend getting rid of most email notifications as well. Again, the goal is to make it so that you engage with a social media site when you decide to, not when it tries to grab your attention. From my own experience, if you turn off all your notifications and stop going to Facebook, they'll start sending you emails about random stuff ("Did you know someone you haven't talked to since high school just posted a picture of her cat?") that are meant to draw you back in. You can find a link to unsubscribe from such notifications in the email they send you.

3) Find alternatives: Very often, we are getting something at least a little worthwhile out of social media, and if we just quit it cold turkey, we'll miss that benefit. Maybe instagram was the main way you encountered beauty day to day, or Twitter was how you got news. It can be helpful to figure out what any given social media platform was doing for you and find some other ways to get that benefit.

replies.title: Congratulations on your decision to live Lent as it was meant to be
replies.content:
If you are anything like me, you will find very hard to stay out of social media for the full 40 days. Back in the day I quit social media cold turkey, but had to struggle a lot with porn. In the end, I came to the realization that you cannot force yourself to stop and then be Good; instead, by turning into someone better and better the thing you wanted to stop becomes less and less interesting.

Some strategies that may prove useful, in the short term.

1. Software is magical. Whatever device you are using now has a life of its own, and it will not like stop feeding from you. In the past it did help to make a full OS reinstall to cleanse the device. If that is not feasible, at least try to nuke your current browser and install a new one (maybe use the opportunity to try a freer one?).

2. Prepare to fail and plan accordingly. I am very good and keeping up with the plan as long as I keep up with the plan... much less so after the first crack lines begin to show. In the past, it did help to pre-commit to only use a specific, uncomfortable device if I ever was to transgress in my use of restricted content. No easy access will take you a long way, and make it easier to mount a counteroffensive when things start to go sideways.

3. Do not just try [to not do], do [something else]! Vacuums beg to be filled, and if you not choose what goes in the filling another vice may just crawl in as you kick the original one out. This will be specially effective if the new thing that you do fills some of the same needs that the old one was providing. What makes you happy about social media? Connection with loved ones -> write beautiful letters to them. Hearing uplifting stories -> read a book. Learning new, interesting stuff -> enroll in an online course.

4. If not already doing it, start a purification ritual. Our host has provided instructions for some very solid choices. Prayer, blessed candles and cold showers also work.

Actions that have worked for me in avoiding social media and exerting will in the face of technology.

Make the things you are concerned about harder. I only go to social media on a computer. I plug in to ethernet. I log in and if I stop for anything, I log back out. Half the time if you sit down to do/check something on social media, by the time you get up to pee or get a snack, you are done. If you log out, the simple obstruction of logging back in will keep you from logging in frivolously.

Find a few trusted sources of news and check them once or twice a day. Again, log out of the computer and back in each time. Do not keep tabs open. Bookmark anything you think is important (it's unlikely you will.) This has taken news down from a constant stream to a manageable trickle for me. After doing this for a couple weeks, there is so little "new" news each day that I don't always find it worth the effort of logging in and opening the browser.

Wishing you well.

comments.title: Soul vs Spirit
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
From my naive position I associate the word "soul" with humans, animals, beasts. I associate the word "spirit" with earth, air, fire, water. Almost as if "soul" means "able to move around" while "spirit" is tied to a specific location. (Although the idea of a Sylph is certainly mobile.) Almost as if "soul" is more sapient or sentient than "spirit."

Yet the word "spirit" is used with respect to human beings in the Platonic mind, body, spirit trifecta and I have heard it used as a 5th natural element to join the four mentioned.

Can you offer an opinion, definition, or a few research threads to follow?

thank you

replies.title: Re: Soul vs Spirit
replies.content:
Hi Anonymous,

You want to be careful with the word "spirit" as it's used in Platonic writings. In fact, the most difficult part of getting into Platonic philosophy is the problem of vocabulary. Very often, when the word "spirit" is used, it's used to translate the Greek word "thymos." "Thymos" is one of the 3 parts of the soul, as Socrates describes them in the Republic. The other two are "nous" and "epithymia." And this is is where the trouble comes in. "Epithymia" means appetite, and refers to all of your biological drives. But neither "nous" nor "thymos" has an accurate English translation. Often, like I said, "thymos" is translated as "spirit" and "nous" as either "reason" or "mind." And this very quickly leads to a big misunderstanding, because the teaching in the Republic is that nous should rule, with thymos as its guardian and assistant. If you're assuming that "nous" is reason and "thymos" is spirit, that makes it sound like spirit should be subordinated to reason! That's not what Plato is saying at all.

Rather than "spirit" in the sense of "incorporeal soul," thymos means "spirit" in the sense of "a spirited horse" or "You're in high spirits today." A far better English translation is actually "heart," in the sense that that word is used by high school athletic coaches. "The team with the most heart will win!" That makes even more sense if you realize that the ancients and medievals actually located the thymos at the heart, as an energy center-- it's also where we get the name of the thymus gland.

"Nous," meanwhile, includes "reason" in the sense that we use that word, but it means more than that, too. Proclus says that nous also contains 3 parts, arranged hierarchically. The lowest is opinion. The next is discursive reason. And then there is nous proper. This is a higher faculty than reason; when nous is truly functioning, the mind is able to function beyond duality and to directly perceive spiritual realities. FWIW, the only people writing in the English language today who have preserved a really good understanding of the nous are the Eastern Orthodox Christians.

As far as the "mind, body, spirit" trifecta you mention, this should really properly be Body, Soul, Intellect. Or, to give them their Greek names-- Hyle, Psyche, Nous. Soul or Psyche contains all the powers I just named-- Epithymia, Thymos, Nous-- and so at its highest level, the nous level, it reaches beyond itself to the next level of being. Divine beings exist at the level of nous all the time; we don't; the opening of the nous is also called "Developing the mental sheath" in modern occult teaching. (And, of course, there is a level of being beyond the Noetic-- the Henadic, or the level of the One.)

replies.title: Re: Soul vs Spirit
replies.content: I was about to walk you through the Greek words, but u/readoldthings saved me the trouble! The English word "spirit" is used extraordinarily loosely, for things that have very little to do with one another. The use of it as a fifth natural element, for example, is an attempt to find some rough equivalent for the Sanskrit word akasha, which means "sky" or "space" and is the fifth element in the Hindu system of tattvas. Its uses in other contexts refer to even more things -- Holy Spirit, for example, is a translation of the Greek Pneuma to Hagion, and pneuma is yet another concept -- distinct from thymos and also from nous. (It's the Greek word for the life force, equivalent to qi, prana, voor, etc.) So when you find the word "spirit" in an English language source, the gods alone know what it means. ;-)

comments.title: “Cursed” rosary beads?
comments.content:
JMG -  I wanted to pass on a portion of an email I received because something about it rubs me the wrong way, but I can’t put my finger on it.  I wonder what you think is really going on here.  

I have removed any details that would identify the sender:

“It has very recently come to our attention that some plastic rosaries are in circulation in the United States coming from countries such as Africa and the Philippines, which have a curse placed on them.   We have consulted our ... Chaplain, and a reliable exorcist priest to see if such rosaries present a legitimate danger (if they are blessed does this make them safe?  Is this concern real?).   These priests confirmed that such rosaries may indeed have a curse on them, and can present a spiritual danger.  Blessing the rosary will not remove the curse.  Rather, the rosary would need to be burned.  

...   The cursed rosaries contain symbols on the crucifix that are not part of our Catholic faith (one such symbol is a sun located above our Lord on the crucifix).  

If you find one of these rosaries, please either burn it, or give it to a local priest to burn it.   At ..., we are taking an additional measure to have traditionally blessed Benedict medals permanently affixed to our ...  crucifixes.” 

Like I said, there are a few things about this message that bug me, but I can’t put my finger on it. Here’s what I think it could be:

1) They say these beads are cursed, but how do they know? They offer no evidence for this.

2) They claim that “blessing” the beads will not work, but how can they know? They offer no evidence for that either.

3) The closest thing to evidence for a curse they offer is a symbol of a sun. That doesn’t sound too bad to me. Does this strike you as a knee-jerk “Everything Occult is Bad” sort of reaction? It seems like it to me.

4) Do you think they are handling this the right way? If they are correct about the curse, is burning plastic the right course of action? (It seems bad from an environmental perspective, if maybe not an occult perspective.)

replies.title: Re: “Cursed” rosary beads?
replies.content:
Yes, this strikes me as very, very weird. If a blessing in the name of God will not overcome the curse in the beads, then they're claiming that their god is less powerful than the forces of evil. Furthermore, since (iirc) most Catholic rosaries are specifically dedicated to the Virgin, and of all the holy entities in Catholicism she seems to dispense miracles most readily, you'd think that if anyone took one of those "cursed" rosaries and said the rosary with it, it would be cleansed of evil like nobody's business.

As for what's actually behind it, I don't know, but I'll just say that if I was a Satanist and wanted to get devout Catholics to destroy their rosaries, this kind of thing would strike me as very, very clever...

replies.title: Re: “Cursed” rosary beads?
replies.content:
It would depend on why one is burning it. A sacred or blessed object can, if certain conditions present themselves, be burned. As an example, last year (or maybe it was 2020), a priest in the Archdiocese of New Orleans was caught, along with two dominatrixes, recording a pornographic scene on a parish altar. The archbishop, after reviewing the report of what occurred on the altar, decided it was best to deconsecrate the altar, burn the now-ordinary table, and simply consecrate a new altar for that parish.

Less extreme, a damaged crucifix or rosary could, if repairs are not feasible or possible, be respectfully burned or buried. If the sacred object being burned is made of wood, I'd recommend keeping some of the ash. I've found that such ash, such as from the blessed palm leafs from Palm Sunday, are magically potent. I've used it to create holy water, as well to sprinkle in areas of my home to ward off malign spirits.

If one does decide to burn a blessed object as an act of desecration, well, the Bible is full of descriptions of God's wrath.

replies.title: Re: “Cursed” rosary beads?
replies.content: It is normal and respectful to dispose of blessed items by burning them. We have a lot of blessed objects around-- everything from icons to candles to flowers, eggs, and bread. The rule is, if it's past its use-by date (eg the flowers are dead, the easter eggs have reached old age in the fridge, or your toddler spat out the blessed bread on the carpet...), you can give it a respectful burial, or you can burn it. You definitely should not throw it in the trash. Some churches even hold an annual community fire so that people can bring all their old paper items with iconography printed on them to burn, as many of us feel weird about throwing those in the trash, but we get inundated with them in the form of religious junk mail, church calendars, greeting cards, funeral prayer cards, etc. A lot of those aren't even technically blessed, but...  even with coffee stains on it, I can't bear to have St. Nicholas reproaching me from his sunday-school-goodie-bag bookmark, in the kitchen trash. Better to go make a little fire in the grill, and then sweep the ashes into a flowerbed.

replies.title: Re: “Cursed” rosary beads?

Demons and malevolent forces aren't more powerful than gods, and cursed objects can be blessed and, if need be, exorcized.  It really isn't much different than blessings or exorcisms performed for human beings.

replies.title: Re: Green Man
replies.content:
Well, first of all, do keep in mind that we're talking about a fictional character!

The gimmick that's central to my tentacle novels is that the Great Old Ones are the reality behind the gods of all the old polytheist faiths. That's not what I actually believe, you know -- in this world, afflicted as it is by a shortage of shoggoths and night-gaunts, the reality behind the god-images of polytheism isn't something human beings are capable of comprehending, which is why we use the god-images.

Within the setting of the Haliverse, each of the Great Old Ones can take on whatever forms they choose -- they have their standard forms, but they're not constrained by them, which is why Shub-Ne'hurrath can show up as an ugly old woman with stringy hair, or as a titanic figure with goat legs, or as a vast writhing mass of tentacles and eyes, or, well, any other form she chooses. In that fictive setting, the names and forms given by humans to the gods are partly symbolism and partly a reflection of how the Great Old Ones happened to show up in any given context.

comments.title: Horary numerology questions
comments.content:
I've been playing around with horary numerology and had some questions - and these aren't necessarily directed to Mr. Greer, so everyone feel free to chime in.

1) I'm having trouble interpreting 9. On the one hand, the karma aspect seems to say look to your past experience on the subject. On the other, though, the completion aspect seems to say you should bring things to an end. Do these seem reasonable? Are there other interpretations I'm missing?

2) I've asked both "should I do..." and "is ... true" types of questions. All of the number summaries in "Numerology: The Time Number" post are geared toward action so they seem much more applicable to the first kind of question. Any suggestions for interpreting the second type of question? Or perhaps is horary numerology not suited to questions of fact unrelated to action? (An example of the first question would be "should I join the Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose?" and an example of the second would be "is anyone in my family vaccine injured?" or perhaps "will anyone in my family be vaccine injured?")

3) Horary numerology is nice in that you get a very quick answer - sometimes I can even calculate the time number in my head. I'm wondering if there's a downside to this, though: some days I find myself thinking of all kinds of questions and I worry that I could overdo things. I'm also wary of thinking of a question a minute or so after calculating the time number for another question, as I pretty much know the answer already and fear I could unconsciously manipulate the question into getting the answer I want. Are there any guidelines around overusing divination in general?

replies.title: Re: Horary numerology questions
replies.content:
1) I tend to treat 9s as "it is what it is, deal with it" answers.

2) Hmm! That's an interesting point. I haven't tried yes/no questions using horary numerology. On first principles, I'd expect 1, 6, and 8 to be a firm yes, 2, 4, and 5 to be a firm no, 3 to mean "you're missing the point," 7 to mean "rethink the question," and 9 to mean "that's all over and done with, so stop fretting about it." But that's a guess.

3) "Never do it more than once in two hours" is classic good advice. "Never do it more than once for the same question" is more of the same.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Last week, a commenter proposed an SOP working to invoke the Powers of the Sphinx according to each of the elements: To Know, to Will, to Dare and to Be Silent.

I have been thinking a lot about it, and I tried to assign Ogham fews to each of those four qualities.

To Know would be Phagos. I toyed long and hard whether To Know would correspond to Nuin, which is the few of communication but also to me the proper naming of things, which involves knowledge of them. But then I remembered that somewhere there was discussion that To Know was, in German, about the "wissen" type of knowledge and not "kennen". Therefore Phagos, the few of learning, study and knowledge, seems to fit.

To Will would be Duir. The few of strength and power, this seems to work with the original poster's proposal and I think it fits nicely: strength of will. Duir is Fire of Fire, the unifying tendency of Fire is itself unified into a focus that is as strong as it is precise, and is strengthened by further precision.

To Dare would be Eadha: the few of courage in the face of the odds, to strike out and do something even if you're not sure of victory.

To Be Silent was a hard one, but I settled on Huathe. The few of reserve, that is, silence. Patiently waiting, knowing and planning what must be done, but waiting for the right time to do it. Or waiting as an exercise in resisting the impulse to act, as action may not be what is called for.

It was tempting to try and use fews from each of the aicmes because they correspond to elements, such as using an Air few to correspond to Knowledge, but the meanings of themselves seem to be more precise when linked with the Sphinx's powers.

What do you think?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I think this is exactly the sort of thing that a student of Druidical magic should be doing. Go ye forth and keep at it!

replies.title: Re: Southern Hemisphere
replies.content: It's a classic mistake to try to establish principles in advance of experiment. What I've read is that some people -- for example, the Golden Dawn temple in New Zealand -- took Northern Hemisphere rituals exactly as written, changed nothing, and got good results. Other people made various modifications, and also got good results. It may take a great deal more experimentation to try to sort out what should be changed and what should not.

replies.title: Re: Besetting sins
replies.content:
I can't speak to Heathenry, as I'm not a Heathen and don't know their community close up. I know as much as I do about Christianity because so many Christians like to push it on other people at high volume, and even so I don't claim to have a complete list of the religion's problems. As for Druidry, I don't know if I can give a complete list there either, but two come immediately to mind.

1) A different kind of dishonesty. Druids don't make up nasty stories about other people but oh, boy, do we like telling whoppers about ourselves! Retrospective recruitment -- people in the past who weren't Druids being roped into membership -- is one common habit, backdating current habits to the dawn of time is another, and let's not forget the "Druids knew everything" school of nonhistory; I think the record here was set by the Victorian Druid writer who insisted that the ancient Druids had steam engines, but the competition is fierce.

2) The kind of spiritual pride that leads to amoeba politics and infinite schism. It's really a wonder that among the two million or so Druids in the world, there aren't at least two million and one different Druid orders, each insisting that they and they alone have the One True Druidry and denouncing everyone else's failings.

If you can avoid those two, you're doing pretty well...

comments.title: Geometrics, Synaesthesia, Visual Weirdness
comments.content:
Apologies for the long preamble.

I have a lot of garden-variety visual processing anomalies-- phosphenes, persistent afterimages, object-auras, visual snow, ripples in things that shouldn't ripple, that sort of thing, plus some synaesthesia that lets certain sounds register as visual stimuli. Immature autistic nervous system or something. When I was a child, of course, I learned not to mention it to anybody if it wasn't strictly "real" in the "it has a location in physical space and I can touch it" sense. Growing up, I did not stop seeing these things, but I did get better at tuning them out, not paying them much attention, and maintaining the, uh... state of consciousness (??) that lets one get by in the material world. My childhood was pretty much a constant mild psychedelia, and being socially and practically functional meant training myself to sort of... step sideways into the material plane and focus on the physical in a way that clears away most of the visual "noise", which can get somewhat distracting when, say, driving, reading, or trying to follow a conversation.

As an adult, my default state is more normal. But I can still "drop out" or "shake loose" from that state with a little effort, by skipping a couple meals, by sitting still and focusing intensely on a small object, through meditative prayer, or sometimes by sort of mentally stepping back from my usual locus of perception (like, around the eyes, where most of us feel like our "selves" are located"). I am wondering if you or the intrepid members of the commentariat here might be able to shed some light on a few of these things for me, or furnish a better vocabulary for talking about them. When I was twenty, I was accustomed to thinking about these phenomena in strictly neurological terms. Just: "something's misfiring in the brain case". At forty, I'm no longer certain about that. There are some things I see only when I pray. They don't seem particularly meaningful, but... if it's just neurons, why should intense prayer "look" different from any other intense concentration? Once in a while, something swishes by that seems to have a bit of life or personality of its own. No idea what to do with those-- can't tell if they're friendly or not, so mostly ignore. Anyway, some questions:

1) Is there an existing vocabulary for this stuff? The best I've found is texts that talk about psychedelic experiences. They describe a few of the things quite well, and give some words for it (eg "phosphenes"), but I feel weird about using that vocabulary, as I'm an extremely clean-living person and have never done any kind of drugs. It's frustrating that I have to flail around and grasp at psychonaut culture just to have words for my default way of seeing the world. But I notice some discussions here brush on things that sound... familiar or related. Do these things fit in the astral/etheric/spiritual paradigm? If so, where? Are there any texts that address it?

2) The act of "stepping sideways" into my childhood-normal perceptual mode also makes the physical world seem very thin and insubstantial. But individual things in it will often seem *more* real at the same time, in a paradoxical sort of way. It's like the concrete, the trees, the tile, the bricks, the things that should be most solid and hurt a lot if you run into them (no I don't try to bash through them!) could just as easily be tissue-paper replicas. They lose their sense of *mass*. But at the same time living things in particular can acquire a more intense sense of (and here I'm flailing for words again, help!) "thing-ness". A tree, a blade of grass... less substantial, but more *present*. As I age, I fall prey far less often to the idea that I'm super-extra-special and things that happen to me must never have happened to anyone else, ever ;) Surely there is a word for this, and other people who have described it. Can anybody point me in the right direction here?

2a) A side-note on this state of consciousness: it also makes me, for most practical purposes, invisible. I'm not claiming I wouldn't show up on a camera or anything wacky like that. I'm obviously still there bouncing visible-spectrum radiation off my surfaces ;) But people tend not to notice me, sometimes in quite startling ways. I was once left in a library after the staff all went home in the evening, because I was so absorbed in using the microfilm viewer that nobody noticed me. Fortunately fire safety crash-bars mean they can't lock you in! This suggests to me that even "normal" people's perception isn't 100% based in the strictly-material world. At least some part of people "seeing" you or not, depends on your state of consciousness. Yes? No? Please say other people here has experienced this. Anybody know more about it?

3)Geometry. How is geometry related to perception? It's clearly something that exists "out there" in the non-physical space, but which is deeply, inextricably, related to the physical world. I'm saying that badly. When I wake in the morning, and turn on a light, I get a flash of luminescent repeating geometric patterns. Never the same one twice, but whoever runs the projector on the backs of my eyelids seems to like fish-scale patterns and interlocking circles a lot. Occasionally things that would make really fun tile floors. In addition, when I focus intensely on some small object, particularly in nature, the peripheral field erupts into geometric patterns. I'm not seeing anything that isn't actually there, but, for example, close-cropped grass invariably resolves itself into diamond patterns. Individual blades of grass, little leaves, pine needles and whatnot become suddenly more prominent, and form distinct lines-- and there it is, a whole field neatly arranged into a grid like slightly-stretched chainlink fence. What is that? Am I imposing order on randomness, or perceiving an underlying order in something that only seems random? Is there any way to tell?

--regular commenter, but as this is a somewhat ticklish subject for me (if you can't see why, try asking the guy next to you why the wall is so sparkly and see how he reacts!), we will be temporarily anonymous. Y'all are generally a polite crowd willing to contemplate oddball things, so, fingers crossed someone has more info than I do.

comments.title: Sortilege Numerology
comments.content:
I think I've thought of a way to do a sortilege version of horary numerology. I haven't had a chance to try it out, but I'm sharing it here before time runs out:

Roll 1d10. If you get 1-9, that's your answer. If you roll 10, roll again add 9 to the new result. Reduce if needed. So rolling 10 followed by 5 gives you 9 + 5 = 14, which reduces to 5. Keeping rolling and adding another 9 (so 18 after the second roll, and so on) as long as you keep getting 10's. (This is a variant of an "exploding die" in tabletop roleplaying games.)

The exploding part makes it possible to generate the master numbers, but they'll be rare. If you're not using master numbers, don't worry about adding on rerolls, just reroll.

replies.title: Re: Sortilege Numerology
replies.content: Hmm! Give it a try and see how it works. At least in theory, it should work.
